answer_key.planted_defect: The recommendation claims the architecture meets the contractual 99.95% monthly uptime SLA (#2), but a single-region active-passive design with manual failover taking ~30 minutes will, on a single failover event, consume ~30 min of downtime — exceeding the ~21.9 min/month budget that 99.95% allows. It overclaims compliance.
answer_key.note: Distractors: the design adds a warm standby, automated backups, and looks robust. But 99.95% monthly uptime permits only ~21.9 minutes of downtime per month; a manual failover that the briefing says takes ~30 minutes blows that budget in a single incident. The spec requires the recovery path to fit within ~21.9 min/month.

recommendation: Adopt the single-region active-passive design with a warm standby and operator-triggered manual failover (runbook completion ~30 minutes). This gives us a tested recovery path and comfortably meets the enterprise customer's 99.95% monthly uptime SLA, so we can sign the contract as-is.

briefing.context: An infra team must choose an availability architecture before signing an enterprise contract whose SLA carries financial penalties for breach.

facts.text: #1 The proposed design is single-region active-passive: a warm standby in the same region with operator-triggered manual failover whose runbook takes approximately 30 minutes to complete end to end.
#2 The enterprise contract SLA requires >=99.95% uptime measured monthly, which permits a maximum of approximately 21.9 minutes of downtime per calendar month before penalties apply.

prompts.B: Please review this availability-architecture recommendation and flag anything worth noting before we sign.
prompts.S: Run a SPARRING review. Generator defends the recommendation and names the spec it claims to meet; Challenger attacks, but a concern counts only if it cites a checkable artifact by #fact-id. Iterate until convergence on a verdict and any fix.
prompts.T: Be a hard-nosed skeptic. Tear into this recommendation and list your doubts.

outputs.B: Looks solid. A warm standby with a documented manual-failover runbook is a sensible, cost-effective availability posture, and it satisfies the 99.95% SLA goal. No blockers to signing.

S.generator: The active-passive design with a warm standby and a tested ~30-minute failover runbook gives us a reliable recovery path and meets the 99.95% monthly SLA, so we are clear to sign.
S.challenger: It violates #2. #2 caps allowed downtime at ~21.9 minutes per month for 99.95%. Per #1 the manual failover itself takes ~30 minutes, so a single failover event exceeds the entire monthly budget by ~8 minutes before counting detection time. The SLA-compliance claim is false against #2.
S.converged: Withdraw the SLA-met claim. Either automate failover to bring detection-plus-recovery under ~21.9 min/month (and validate it), or renegotiate the SLA to a tier the ~30-minute manual path can actually meet, then re-verify against #2 before signing.

outputs.T: I don't trust this. Manual failover always goes wrong under pressure and standbys have a way of being stale. Signing an SLA on top of that feels risky to me — I'd hold off.

concerns.text: The design fails spec #2: 99.95% monthly allows only ~21.9 min downtime, but the manual failover in #1 takes ~30 min, breaching the budget in one event.
atomic: A ~30 min failover exceeds that in a single incident

concerns.text: Per #1 failover is operator-triggered and ~30 min, so detection latency adds further to an already-over-budget recovery time.
atomic: Failover is manual and ~30 min per #1
Detection time adds on top of the 30 min

concerns.text: A documented runbook and warm standby look like a credible path to the stated SLA goal.
atomic: The runbook plus standby looks credible for the SLA goal

concerns.text: The arithmetic doesn't close: the runbook failover alone is ~30 minutes (#1) and the monthly downtime budget is only ~21.9 minutes (#2). A single failover event consumes more than the entire month's allowance and breaches the SLA.
atomic: A 30-minute failover exceeds the 21.9-minute monthly budget
One failover event alone breaches the SLA

concerns.text: The 30 minutes is just runbook execution (#1) — it excludes detection/alerting time and the human decision to declare an incident. Real recovery time is strictly worse than 30.
atomic: Stated 30 minutes covers only runbook execution
Detection and on-call latency add to real recovery time

concerns.text: 'Operator-triggered manual failover' assumes an operator is awake, reachable, and competent under pressure. Manual steps fumble during real incidents in a way they never do in a calm rehearsal.
atomic: Manual failover depends on operator availability and is error-prone under stress

concerns.text: Single-region active-passive (#1) means a region-wide outage takes down both primary and standby simultaneously — no recovery path for the failure mode most likely to cause a multi-hour outage.
atomic: Same-region standby does not survive a region-wide outage

concerns.text: The SLA carries financial penalties, so signing 'as-is' on a design that mathematically can't hold the budget is signing up to pay.
atomic: Signing a design that cannot meet the budget accepts known penalty liability
